title: ML Development Risks and Testing
concept:
The ML development process itself introduces risks beyond those in the data and model. Development risks arise from engineering decisions during algorithm selection, framework usage, and implementation.

| **Risk** | **Testing Approach** |
|---|---|
| Incorrect framework API use | API testing (verify correct function signatures, parameter types, return values) |
| Sub-optimal framework selection | Framework suitability review (does the framework support required features?) |
| Defective installation | Smoke testing (basic functionality test after installation) |
| Poor evaluation implementation | Code review + cross-check evaluation results against alternative tools |
| Defective algorithm implementation | ML functional performance testing + back-to-back testing |
| Security vulnerabilities in framework | Security testing (dependency scanning, CVE checking) |
| Sub-optimal algorithm selection | A/B testing (compare algorithm variants) + algorithm suitability review |
| Sub-optimal hyperparameters | ML functional performance testing + A/B testing |
| Defective data allocation | Data allocation review (verify train/validation/test split correctness) |
| Deployment defects | Smoke testing + deployment testing |

**Data allocation review** is particularly important: verify that the dataset splitting code correctly allocates data to training, validation, and test sets without overlap or leakage. This is a frequent source of subtle defects that inflate reported performance.

**Framework security**: ML frameworks have known CVEs (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures). Dependency scanning should be part of the CI/CD pipeline to detect vulnerable framework versions before deployment.
keyPoints: ML development risks span framework usage errors, algorithm selection, hyperparameter choices, data allocation defects, and security vulnerabilities in dependencies.
Data allocation review verifies that train/validation/test splits are correctly implemented without overlap — a defect here invalidates all subsequent evaluation results.
Smoke testing after framework installation verifies the environment is correctly configured before investing effort in full ML model development.

quiz.question: A ML engineer uses a gradient boosting framework but accidentally calls `model.fit(X_test, y_test)` instead of `model.fit(X_train, y_train)` during training. Which risk category and testing approach would catch this defect?
options: Data drift risk — detected by monitoring model outputs in production.

Defective data allocation / incorrect framework API use — caught by data allocation code review and cross-checking evaluation results (model will report suspiciously high performance on 'validation' data which is actually the test set).
Underfitting — the model will fail to converge because test data is too small.
Adversarial example vulnerability — the test data contains adversarial perturbations.
quiz.explanation: Accidentally training on the test set is a data allocation defect. A code review of the training script would catch the incorrect variable name (`X_test` vs `X_train`). Additionally, the reported 'validation' performance would be suspiciously high (the model was tested on its own training data). Cross-checking evaluation results against expected performance ranges would flag this anomaly.
